content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 9, 2004

Kinde Durkee, Treasurer
Westly 2006 Committee
Durkee & Associates
601 S. Glenoaks Blvd, Suite 211
Burbank, CA 91502

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-182

Dear Ms. Durkee:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Westly 2006
Committee regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

May the Westly 2006 Committee accept a contribution of stock?

CONCLUSION

Yes, however, the stock must be converted to cash prior to expenditure.

FACTS

An individual wishes to make a non-monetary contribution to the Westly 2006
Committee by transferring ownership of shares of stock. The total value (on the day of
transfer) of the shares would not exceed the individual's contribution limit to the
committee.

ANALYSIS

There is nothing in the Act that would prohibit the acceptance of stock from an
individual to a committee as a non-monetary contribution. However, the Act does place

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-04-182
Page No. 2

restrictions on the amount of contributions received per election from an individual to a
candidate for state office. (Section 85301.)

The Westly 2006 Committee is established to reelect State Controller Steve
Westly. Section 85301 prohibits the committee from receiving a contribution from an
individual that exceeds $5,300 per election. When a committee receives a non-monetary
contribution, the committee must value that contribution at what it would cost the
committee to purchase the same item or service on the open market at fair market value.
(Section 82025.5.) Thus, when the committee receives the stock, it must value that stock
at what it would cost the committee to make such a purchase on the day it was received.

Please note that pursuant to regulation 18524, a candidate controlled committee
may not transfer campaign funds to common or preferred stock. (See Sutton Advice
Letter, No. I-96-228.) Therefore, although a committee may accept stock as a non-
monetary contribution, the funds must be converted to cash and deposited in the
candidate's campaign bank account prior to expenditure. Finally, the Act requires that
candidates and committees disclose sources of campaign contributions. Please see the
enclosed advice letter (Sutton, supra) for instructions on how to report the receipt of
stock as a contribution.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kevin S. Moen, PhD
Political Reform Consultant II
Technical Assistance Division

Enclosure

I:\AdviceLtrs\04-182
